problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem A (continuous): Rising ocean temperatures push Scottish herring and mackerel out of their habitat. The team had to (1) predict 50-year migration paths of both species, (2) forecast when fish leave a no-refrigeration fishing company's reachable range with best/worst cases, (3) recommend business strategy (relocate ports, buy refrigerated boats), (4) assess the impact of fishing entering foreign territorial waters (Iceland, Norway), and (5) write a 1-2 page magazine article. The paper, ID 2003485, treats temperature as the driver and reduces the biology to fixed thermal-tolerance bands (mackerel 8-16°C, herring 12-18°C).
models_used: NOAA data pipeline (1980-2019): bounding-box + sentinel-value (-99.99) filtering, hourly-to-yearly aggregation, discard locations with <50% year coverage (>=20 of 40 yrs) -> 54 representative grid points
Per-location temperature-vs-year curve fitting via MATLAB lsqcurvefit with custom basis F = x1*t^2 + x2*(sin t + cos t) + x3, extrapolated 1980->2070

Spatial interpolation of the 54 fitted points across the whole study region: compared linear/cubic/nearest/v4 (gridata 'v4' biharmonic) and selected v4; visualized as 3D surfaces + filled contour maps

Migration-route extraction: read isotherm bands matching each species' thermal tolerance off contour maps, pick discrete points nearest Scotland, then least-squares polynomial fit of latitude vs longitude to draw the route

Markov chain ('Macleaf/Mark leaf' model) on temperature growth rate discretized into 5 states (rapid rise >=0.03, slow rise, stable, slow decline, rapid decline <=-0.03); built one-step transition matrix from adjacent-year state counts, propagated state probabilities to find best/worst exit years
Fishing-range geometry: 1.5-day salting freshness x 10 knots (18.52 km/h) -> 666.72 km max radius; spherical great-circle distance (acos formula, R=6378.137 km) from port to fish school
Port relocation optimization over 25 Scottish ports: Plan A minimizes summed distance to fish over 50 yrs (-> Orkney); Plan B re-optimizes every decade (Orkney->Stornoway->Scrabster->Lerwick)

Mahalanobis distance (sample-to-population) to test whether fish positions fall inside Iceland/Norway territorial-sea point clouds; threshold calibrated empirically, validated with apparent + cross misjudgment rates (0.1923, 0.24)
Cross-validation models: BP neural network used to independently check the Problem 2 crisis year (~20 yrs, between best/worst); AHP scoring model proposed to rank Plans A vs B
key_techniques: Driver-reduction: collapse a complex ecological question into one governing variable (temperature) plus fixed species thermal bands, making the whole problem tractable end-to-end
Two-stage fit-then-interpolate: fit time series independently per location, THEN interpolate across space per year, keeping temporal extrapolation and spatial filling as separate clean steps
Reading model output off contour maps to seed the next model (isotherm bands -> migration waypoints -> polynomial route): chaining visual intermediate results into downstream quantitative steps

Discretizing a continuous rate into a small finite state space to justify a Markov chain, turning 'how fast will it warm' into a tractable transition-probability question that naturally yields best/worst scenarios
Operationalizing the 'best/worst case' deliverable as max/min over Markov state-probability outcomes rather than hand-waving
Independent cross-checks per sub-problem (BP NN, AHP, real published migration data, misjudgment rates) used purely as validation rather than as primary models
Tying every abstract result back to a concrete business decision (which port, which year to buy refrigerated boats, negotiate with Iceland/Norway)
why_it_won: "Judges reward a complete, decision-oriented narrative, and this paper delivers all five deliverables with a clean problem-to-decision chain and concrete numbers (exit by 2032 at 30% worst case / 2050 at 21.91% best case; relocate Orkney->Lerwick; buy refrigerated boats by 2050; herring 29 yrs in Icelandic waters, mackerel 11 yrs in Norwegian). Its strongest move is empirical validation: Problem 1's predicted route is checked against the actual published herring/mackerel migration and matches, which buys enormous judge trust early. It then independently cross-validates each later sub-problem with a DIFFERENT method (BP neural net for the crisis year, AHP scoring for strategy ranking, apparent/cross misjudgment rates for the Mahalanobis classifier), signaling rigor without over-claiming. The modeling is workmanlike but the engineering is thorough and reproducible (full MATLAB appendices), every result maps to an actionable recommendation, and the magazine article is genuinely magazine-like (£5 banknote mackerel hook, plain-language framing) rather than a rehashed abstract."
section_highlights: "Strong structure choices worth copying: (1) Narrative section titles that double as findings ('Fish Are Swimming Out of the Control of the Fishing Company', 'Can't Steal Others Small Fish') keep judges oriented. (2) A short flowchart opens every problem section (Figs 4, 11, 13, 16), so the reader sees the method before the math. (3) A dedicated Model Preparation section front-loads all real-world constants (thermal bands, boat speed, 1.5-day freshness, port coordinates) so later sections stay clean. (4) Results are always given as concrete tables (migration coords per decade, exit-year probabilities, port-to-fish distances per decade) tied to a labeled map. (5) Section 8 'Error/Sensitivity Analysis' is actually a per-problem validation log using independent methods plus real data, which reads as honesty. (6) The magazine article uses a vivid local hook (mackerel on the new £5 Scottish note, Scotland landing 60% of UK catch with 8.4% of population)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Biology is over-simplified: fixed constant thermal-tolerance bands, no salinity/oxygen/pH/prey/spawning despite those columns being collected; Assumption 1 literally freezes species preferences for 50 years

The temporal basis F = a*t^2 + b*(sin t + cos t) + c is ad hoc with no physical justification, and a quadratic extrapolated to 2070 can diverge unrealistically; sin/cos of raw year values is near-meaningless

Data-integrity red flags in the appendix: data4(:,1) is overwritten by rand() twice (Appendix 2) and the Markov state-counting in Appendix 5 has a copy-paste bug (the 5th category condition duplicates 'j>5 && j<11'), so some reported probabilities may not reflect the described method

The Markov 'Macleaf/Mark leaf' model is mislabeled and under-specified; transition probabilities come from only ~40 yearly transitions per location, a thin sample, and stationarity is assumed under a non-stationary warming trend
Best/worst cases are defined as extreme outcomes but reported with mid-range probabilities (30%, 21.91%) that aren't true bounds; no confidence interval on exit year

Mahalanobis 'critical standard' threshold is set 'through a large number of experiments' (i.e., tuned by hand) rather than from a principled cutoff; using lat/lon directly with a covariance from a coastline point cloud is geometrically questionable

Plans A/B optimize on planar/great-circle distance only; AHP weighting for strategy selection is proposed but never actually computed; no real cost figures for relocation or new boats despite framing it as a business decision

Sensitivity analysis is shallow: only fishing-boat speed is flagged as a shortcoming; no systematic perturbation of thermal bands, growth-rate thresholds (the 0.03 cutoff), or the v4 interpolation choice
reusable_patterns: Pipeline skeleton for any spatio-temporal forecast problem: clean/aggregate raw data -> drop low-coverage sites -> fit per-site time series -> interpolate across space per time slice -> visualize as 3D surface + contour -> read decision-relevant bands off the contours

Turn an open-ended 'predict the future' question into a finite-state Markov chain by discretizing a rate of change into a handful of states; this cheaply yields best/worst scenarios with attached probabilities
Deliver a 'best and worst case' requirement as max/min over a probabilistic model's outcomes, not as guesses
Validate the headline result against real published data when it exists (here, the actual fish migration route) - it is the single most credibility-building move available
Cross-check each sub-problem with a deliberately different method (NN, AHP, classifier misjudgment rate) and present it in a unified Error/Validation section
Front-load a 'Model Preparation' section that fixes every real-world constant and geographic input, keeping the modeling sections uncluttered
Open each problem section with a small flowchart and use finding-as-title headings so a skimming judge always knows the method and the conclusion

Operational-constraint geometry: convert a perishability/speed limit into a hard reachability radius (freshness_days x speed), then drive relocation/equipment decisions off whether targets fall inside it
Write the magazine deliverable around a concrete local hook and a striking statistic, in lay language, not as a reworded abstract
